content.body: Chemical bonds form when atoms interact to achieve a stable electron configuration — typically a full outer shell (octet rule, 8 electrons). There are three main types of chemical bonds: ionic, covalent, and metallic. The type of bond determines the physical and chemical properties of the substance.

IONIC BONDING:
Formed between a METAL and a NON-METAL
Metal loses electrons → becomes positive ion (cation)
Non-metal gains electrons → becomes negative ion (anion)
Opposite charges attract → ionic bond

Electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions

Example:
Na (2,8,1) → loses 1e⁻ → Na⁺ (2,8)
Cl (2,8,7) → gains 1e⁻ → Cl⁻ (2,8,8)
Na⁺ and Cl⁻ form NaCl (sodium chloride = table salt)

Properties of Ionic Compounds:
• High melting and boiling points (strong electrostatic forces)
• Solid at room temperature (giant ionic lattice structure)
• Conduct electricity when DISSOLVED in water or MOLTEN (ions free to move)
• Do NOT conduct when solid (ions fixed in lattice)
• Usually soluble in water
• Hard but brittle (layers shift under pressure, like charges repel)

content.title: Dot-and-Cross Diagrams
content.body:
Sodium chloride (NaCl) formation:
Na has 1 outer electron — it TRANSFERS to Cl
Cl has 7 outer electrons — receives 1 to complete octet
Na becomes Na⁺ (like Ne configuration: 2,8)
Cl becomes Cl⁻ (like Ar configuration: 2,8,8)

Magnesium oxide (MgO):
Mg (2,8,2) → loses 2e⁻ → Mg²⁺
O (2,6) → gains 2e⁻ → O²⁻
Formula: MgO (charges balance 2+ and 2-)

questions.question: Ionic bonds form between:
questions.explanation: Ionic bonds form when a metal (donates electrons) bonds with a non-metal (accepts electrons).

questions.question: Na loses 1 electron. The ion formed is:
questions.explanation: Losing one electron gives Na a positive charge of +1. Ion = Na⁺.

questions.question: Ionic compounds conduct electricity when:
questions.explanation: Ionic compounds only conduct when ions are free to move — in molten state or solution. Solid ionic compounds have fixed ions and don't conduct.

questions.question: Why do ionic compounds have high melting points?
options: Strong electrostatic attraction between ions requires much energy to break
questions.answer: Strong electrostatic attraction between ions requires much energy to break
questions.explanation: The giant ionic lattice has strong forces between all ions. Much energy is needed to overcome these forces and separate the ions.

questions.question: Magnesium ion Mg²⁺ is formed when Mg:
questions.explanation: Mg has 2 outer electrons. It loses both to form Mg²⁺ with a full outer shell.

questions.question: Chloride ion Cl⁻ has the same electron configuration as:
questions.explanation: Cl⁻ has 2,8,8 — same as argon (Ar). Both have 18 electrons.

questions.question: Formula of calcium oxide: Ca²⁺ and O²⁻
questions.explanation: Ca²⁺ (+2) and O²⁻ (-2). Charges balance at ratio 1:1. Formula = CaO.

questions.question: Ionic compounds are brittle because:
options: Layers shift under pressure — like charges align and repel
questions.answer: Layers shift under pressure — like charges align and repel
questions.explanation: When force is applied, ionic layers shift. Like charges end up adjacent — massive repulsion causes the crystal to shatter.

questions.question: The octet rule states atoms are most stable with:
questions.explanation: Most atoms are most stable with 8 electrons in their outer shell (except H and He which need 2).

questions.question: Which is an ionic compound?
questions.explanation: NaCl (sodium chloride) is formed between Na (metal) and Cl (non-metal) — it is ionic.

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body:
COVALENT BONDING:
Formed between TWO NON-METALS
Atoms SHARE pairs of electrons (not transfer)
Each atom provides one electron to each shared pair
Single bond: 1 shared pair (—)
Double bond: 2 shared pairs (=)
Triple bond: 3 shared pairs (≡)

content.title: Simple Covalent Molecules
content.body:
Hydrogen (H₂): H–H (1 single bond)
Chlorine (Cl₂): Cl–Cl
Water (H₂O): 2 O–H bonds, O has 2 lone pairs
Ammonia (NH₃): 3 N–H bonds, N has 1 lone pair
Methane (CH₄): 4 C–H bonds, tetrahedral shape
Carbon dioxide (CO₂): O=C=O (2 double bonds)

Properties of Simple Covalent Compounds:
• Low melting and boiling points (weak intermolecular forces between molecules)
• Usually gases or liquids at room temperature
• Do NOT conduct electricity (no charged particles)
• Insoluble in water (usually) but soluble in organic solvents

content.title: Giant Covalent Structures
content.body:
Some covalent substances form giant lattices:

Diamond (carbon):
• Each C bonded to 4 others (tetrahedral)
• Very hard (all bonds must be broken)
• Very high melting point
• Does NOT conduct electricity

Silicon dioxide (SiO₂) — quartz/sand:
• Giant covalent lattice
• Very hard, high melting point

Graphite (carbon):
• Layers of hexagonal rings
• Conducts electricity (1 free electron per carbon)
• Soft and slippery (layers slide over each other)
• Used as lubricant and in pencils

questions.question: Covalent bonds involve:
questions.explanation: In covalent bonding, non-metal atoms share electron pairs to achieve full outer shells.

questions.question: H₂O has how many covalent bonds?
questions.explanation: Water: H–O–H. Two O–H single covalent bonds.

questions.question: CO₂ structure is:
questions.explanation: Carbon dioxide: two C=O double bonds. Carbon forms 4 bonds total (2 double bonds = 4).

questions.question: Why do simple covalent compounds have low melting points?
options: Weak intermolecular forces between molecules need little energy to overcome
questions.answer: Weak intermolecular forces between molecules need little energy to overcome
questions.explanation: The COVALENT bonds within molecules are strong. But the forces BETWEEN molecules (intermolecular forces) are weak — these are broken on melting.

questions.question: Diamond is hard because:
options: Each carbon forms 4 strong covalent bonds in a giant 3D lattice
questions.answer: Each carbon forms 4 strong covalent bonds in a giant 3D lattice
questions.explanation: Diamond's giant covalent lattice — every atom bonded to 4 others by strong covalent bonds — makes it extremely hard.

questions.question: Graphite conducts electricity because:
options: One electron per carbon is delocalized and free to move
questions.answer: One electron per carbon is delocalized and free to move
questions.explanation: In graphite, each C forms 3 bonds. The 4th electron is delocalized (free) — these mobile electrons conduct electricity.

questions.question: Which is a covalent compound?
questions.explanation: H₂O is formed between two non-metals (H and O) — covalent bonding.

questions.question: Methane (CH₄) contains:
questions.explanation: Carbon forms 4 single bonds. Methane: C bonded to 4 hydrogen atoms by 4 single C–H bonds.

questions.question: Why doesn't simple covalent H₂O conduct electricity?
options: No free ions or electrons to carry charge
questions.answer: No free ions or electrons to carry charge
questions.explanation: Covalent molecules have no charged particles (ions or free electrons), so they cannot conduct electrical current.

questions.question: Silicon dioxide (SiO₂) has a high melting point because:
options: It has a giant covalent lattice with many strong bonds to break
questions.answer: It has a giant covalent lattice with many strong bonds to break
questions.explanation: SiO₂ (sand/quartz) has a giant covalent structure. Enormous energy is needed to break all the strong covalent bonds throughout the lattice.

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body: Covalent bonding involves sharing electron pairs between non-metal atoms. Each shared pair forms one bond.

Covalent bond formation:
Each atom contributes one electron to the shared pair
Single bond: 1 shared pair (H-H)
Double bond: 2 shared pairs (O=O)
Triple bond: 3 shared pairs (N≡N)

Dot-and-cross diagrams show electron arrangement

content.title: Simple Molecular vs Giant Covalent
content.body:
Simple molecular (e.g. H₂O, CO₂, CH₄):
• Low melting/boiling points
• Do not conduct electricity
• Weak intermolecular forces

Giant covalent (e.g. diamond, silicon dioxide):
• Very high melting points (strong C-C covalent bonds throughout)
• Diamond: hardest natural substance, does not conduct electricity
• Graphite: layered structure, delocalised electrons → conducts electricity, soft (layers slide)
• Silicon dioxide (SiO₂): quartz, glass

questions.question: Covalent bonding involves...
questions.explanation: Covalent bonds form when two non-metal atoms each contribute an electron to form a shared pair.

questions.question: Water (H₂O) has how many covalent bonds?
questions.explanation: Each H-O is one covalent bond. Water has 2 O-H bonds (2 shared pairs).

questions.question: Carbon dioxide has...
questions.explanation: CO₂: each oxygen forms a double bond with carbon → O=C=O (2 double bonds, 4 shared pairs total)

questions.question: Diamond has a very high melting point because...
options: Each carbon has 4 strong covalent bonds in 3D network
questions.answer: Each carbon has 4 strong covalent bonds in 3D network
questions.explanation: Diamond: giant covalent lattice where every C is bonded to 4 others. Breaking it requires breaking many strong bonds.

questions.question: Graphite conducts electricity because...
options: It has delocalised electrons in layers
questions.answer: It has delocalised electrons in layers
questions.explanation: In graphite, each C forms 3 bonds (layers). The fourth electron is delocalised → mobile charge carrier → conducts electricity.

questions.question: Simple molecular compounds have low melting points because...
options: Only weak intermolecular forces need to be overcome
questions.answer: Only weak intermolecular forces need to be overcome
questions.explanation: Melting breaks intermolecular forces (not covalent bonds). These van der Waals/H-bond forces are weak → low melting point.

questions.question: Silicon dioxide (SiO₂) has high melting point because...
options: Giant covalent structure with many strong Si-O bonds
questions.answer: Giant covalent structure with many strong Si-O bonds
questions.explanation: SiO₂ (quartz/glass) is a giant covalent structure with many strong Si-O bonds throughout — requires lots of energy to break.

questions.question: Which substance does NOT conduct electricity in any state?
questions.explanation: Diamond has no free electrons or ions. Covalent bonds are all used in fixed C-C bonds — cannot conduct in any state.

questions.question: Nitrogen molecule (N₂) has...
questions.explanation: N≡N: nitrogen forms a triple bond (3 shared pairs) to achieve a full outer shell.

questions.question: Fullerene (C₆₀, Buckminsterfullerene) is...
questions.explanation: Buckyballs (C₆₀) are discrete molecules of 60 carbon atoms forming a sphere — molecular (not giant) covalent structure.

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Chemical Bonding. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Chemical Bonding concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of chemistry.
Core Theory

This section of Chemical Bonding builds on foundational knowledge. In chemistry, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 4.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Chemical Bonding. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 4.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Chemical Bonding. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Chemical Bonding include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Chemical Bonding. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).

Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Chemical Bonding. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).
